label: Classifier-Free Guidance
definition: A sampling trick that makes a conditional diffusion model follow its condition more closely by extrapolating away from the model's own unconditional prediction, with no separate classifier needed. It is the near-universal dial for trading prompt fidelity against diversity in modern generators, and it costs a doubling of sampling compute since the network runs twice per step.

body.text: Classifier-free guidance is the standard trick for making a conditional diffusion model follow its condition more closely, for example to make a text-to-image system adhere tightly to the prompt. A plain conditional model, asked for an image of a specific subject, will often produce something only loosely related, because the condition is just one influence among many on each denoising step. Classifier-free guidance sharpens that influence at sampling time by combining two predictions, one made with the condition and one made without it, and pushing the result in the direction the condition indicates.

It matters because it is the simple, near-universal lever that controls the trade-off between fidelity to the prompt and diversity of the output. Turn the guidance scale up and samples cling more tightly to the condition and look cleaner and more on-prompt; turn it down and you get more varied, sometimes more natural results that stray further from the request. Almost every large text-to-image, text-to-video, and conditional audio system exposes this control, and it is a major reason such systems can be steered so precisely from text. Its predecessor, classifier guidance, required training a separate classifier on noisy data to provide the steering gradient; the classifier-free variant removed that dependency entirely, which is what made it ubiquitous.

The method works by training a single network to operate in two modes. During training the condition is randomly dropped on some fraction of examples, replaced by a null token, so the same model learns both the conditional and the unconditional prediction. At sampling the model is run twice per step, once with the real condition and once with the null, and the final prediction is the unconditional one plus an amplified version of the difference between the two. The guidance scale sets that amplification: zero ignores the condition, one is ordinary conditional sampling, and larger values extrapolate further in the conditional direction. Seen through the lens of a score-based model, this is adding extra weight to the score of the condition given the data, which mathematically explains why it steers generation.

The cost and caveats are worth stating plainly. Because the network is evaluated twice at every denoising step, classifier-free guidance roughly doubles the compute of sampling. Push the guidance scale too high and the benefit reverses, with samples becoming oversaturated, losing fine texture, or collapsing in diversity, so the scale is a hyperparameter tuned per system and per use. It is specific to the conditional generation setting of diffusion models, including the dominant latent diffusion form, and it composes with the surrounding denoising diffusion machinery rather than replacing any of it, acting purely as a modification of how each prediction is formed during sampling.

image.alt: From a field of noise, an unconditional path and a conditional path both run toward a generated image of a horse, meeting at a marked point; labeled unconditional and condition.
image.caption: Classifier-free guidance steers a diffusion model toward a prompt by running the network twice, once with the condition and once without, then extrapolating away from the unconditional prediction. Turning the guidance strength up sharpens how faithfully the image follows the prompt, at some cost to diversity.
